title: Psalms 35
subtitle: Contend with Those Who Contend with Me

sections.header: Verses 1–18 — “Fight Against Those Who Fight Against Me”

heb.paragraph: "Contend (rîbâ), LORD, with those who contend with me" (v.1). A legal-military psalm. David asks God to take up shield and buckler (v.2), to say to his soul "I am your salvation" (v.3). The enemies set traps without cause (v.7), repaid evil for good (v.12), gloated when David stumbled (v.15). David protests his innocence and asks for vindication.

cross.note: David's plea for God to fight his accusers resonates with the Saul narratives, where David refused to strike the LORD's anointed and trusted God to judge between them.
Psalm 109 develops the same imprecatory pattern: the psalmist's enemies repay good with evil. Both psalms combine urgent pleas for justice with affirmations of personal innocence.
Jesus' teaching — 'Blessed are you when people insult you, persecute you and falsely say all kinds of evil against you' — addresses the same situation of unjust accusation that drives Psalm 35.

notes.note: MacArthur: David appeals to God as his divine warrior and defender. The military metaphors — shield, buckler, spear — remind us that God is actively engaged in fighting for his people, not passively watching from heaven.

MacArthur: the contrast between David's compassion and his enemies' cruelty illustrates a principle repeated throughout Scripture: the righteous are often repaid with evil for good, just as Christ would be (1 Pet 3:9).

alter.source: Robert Alter, The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Alter: the military language is relentless — contend, fight, shield, armour, spear, javelin. The psalmist summons God as a warrior who takes up arms on behalf of the defenceless. This is not metaphor; it is battle cry.
Alter: 'when they were ill, I put on sackcloth' — the psalmist's past compassion for his enemies intensifies the injustice of their present cruelty. He mourned for them; they celebrate his suffering.

Calvin: 'Contend with those who contend with me' — Calvin reads this as an appeal to divine justice, not personal vengeance. The psalmist transfers his cause from human courts to the only impartial judge.

Calvin: false witnesses and evil for good — Calvin sees here the pattern of all unjust persecution: the innocent are accused of crimes they did not commit, while their genuine kindness is repaid with cruelty.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: NET: 'Contend' (rîvāh) is legal terminology — to bring a lawsuit, to argue a case. The psalmist asks God to serve as his advocate in a judicial dispute, not merely his protector in battle.

NET: the Hebrew of v.16 is obscure. 'Like the ungodly' may be 'like mockers at a feast' (ḥanpê la'agê mā'ôg). The image may be of parasites who mock their host — false friends who devour hospitality and then ridicule.

Kidner: the request 'Say to me, "I am your salvation"' is remarkable. The psalmist does not ask for deliverance only — he asks to hear God say it. The word of assurance matters as much as the act of rescue.
Kidner: the psalmist's past compassion for his enemies is described in extraordinary detail — fasting, prayer, mourning as for a brother. The contrast with their present betrayal is devastating.

VanGemeren: the warrior-God imagery draws on the Holy War tradition. Yahweh fights for his people — shield, buckler, spear. The psalmist appropriates corporate Israel's war theology for personal deliverance.

VanGemeren: 'Who is like you, Lord?' echoes the Song of the Sea (Exod 15:11). The psalmist's personal rescue recalls Israel's national redemption. Individual and communal salvation share the same divine character.

Goldingay: the net-and-pit imagery invokes poetic justice — let them fall into the traps they set. The principle is not vindictiveness but proportionality: the punishment should match the crime's method.
Goldingay: 'How long, Lord, will you look on?' — the accusation of divine spectatorship is bold. The psalmist charges God not with absence but with inaction — watching suffering without intervening.

sections.header: Verses 19–28 — “Let Those Who Delight in My Vindication Shout for Joy”

heb.paragraph: "Vindicate me in your righteousness (ṣidqâ), LORD my God" (v.24). The psalm moves from plea to confidence. David asks that those who delight in his vindication will praise God continually (v.27). God’s tongue will proclaim his righteousness all day long (v.28).

cross.note: The vow to praise God 'in the great assembly' (v. 18) echoes Psalm 22:22. Both psalms move from desperate plea to confident anticipation of public thanksgiving — lament resolved in worship.
Jesus quotes 'They hated me without reason' (v. 19), applying Psalm 35 (and Ps 69:4) to his own experience. The psalmist's innocent suffering becomes a type of Christ's passion.

notes.note: MacArthur: Jesus directly applied this verse to himself (John 15:25), identifying his own unjust persecution with David's experience. The psalm is both historically Davidic and prophetically messianic.

MacArthur: the psalm concludes with a vision of God's people rejoicing in his justice. The purpose of vindication is not personal satisfaction but corporate praise — God is glorified when righteousness prevails.

alter.source: Robert Alter, The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Alter: 'enemies without cause' (śōn'ê ḥinnām) — the gratuitous nature of their hatred is emphasised. This is not a quarrel with a reason; it is malice for its own sake. Jesus quotes this phrase in John 15:25.

Alter: the psalm ends not with the enemies' destruction but with the vindicated community's praise. 'May those who delight in my vindication shout for joy' — the final note is communal celebration, not solitary revenge.

Calvin: 'Lord, you have seen this; do not be silent' — Calvin notes the paradox: the psalmist accuses God of seeing but not acting. The seeing without acting is harder to bear than ignorance would be.

Calvin: 'The Lord be exalted, who delights in the wellbeing of his servant' — Calvin: God's delight in our welfare is not a concession but a character trait. He is not reluctant to bless; he delights in it.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: NET: 'wink the eye' — qāraṣ 'ayin is a gesture of malicious scheming (cf. Prov 6:13, 10:10). The enemies communicate their plots through coded signals, mocking the psalmist behind his back.
NET: 'Vindicate me in your righteousness' — ṣidqātekā. The appeal is to God's own righteous character, not the psalmist's merit. Divine righteousness becomes the standard by which the case is judged.

Kidner: 'enemies without cause' anticipates Christ's own experience (John 15:25). The innocent sufferer who is hated without reason is a recurring biblical pattern that finds its fullest expression in the cross.
Kidner: the psalm ends with praise 'all day long' — not just at the moment of deliverance but perpetually. Kidner: the tongue that proclaims righteousness replaces the lying tongues of the accusers.

VanGemeren: 'Awake, and rise to my defense!' — the language of divine awakening is anthropomorphic but theologically pointed. God does not sleep (Ps 121:4), but the psalmist's experience feels like divine dormancy.

VanGemeren: 'the Lord delights in the wellbeing (šālôm) of his servant' — this is covenant theology in miniature. God's delight in his people's shalom is not occasional sentiment but covenantal commitment.
Goldingay: the enemies' mockery — 'Aha! Aha!' — is onomatopoeic in Hebrew (he'āḥ he'āḥ). The gleeful exclamation captures the visceral pleasure the wicked take in the righteous person's downfall.

Goldingay: the psalm resolves not with the enemies' punishment but with the community's worship. The vindication is social: those who sided with the psalmist celebrate. Justice is communal, not solitary.

ppl.name: David
ppl.text: David is a key figure in Contend with Those Who Contend with Me.

ppl.name: God
ppl.text: God is a key figure in Contend with Those Who Contend with Me.

ppl.name: Moses↗ People
ppl.text: Moses stands at the centre of the Pentateuch’s narrative — the one man who spoke with God face to face (Exod 33:11; Num 12:8) and mediated the Sinai covenant to Israel. In Leviticus he receives the priestly legislation; in Numbers he leads, intercedes, and ultimately fails at Meribah (Num 20). The writer of Hebrews calls him "faithful in all God’s house" (Heb 3:5) while distinguishing him from Christ, the Son over the house.

ppl.name: Solomon
ppl.text: Solomon is a key figure in Contend with Those Who Contend with Me.

ppl.name: Abraham
ppl.text: Abraham is a key figure in Contend with Those Who Contend with Me.

ppl.text: Jacob is a key figure in Contend with Those Who Contend with Me.

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVmay ruin overtake them by surprise— may the net they hid entangle them, may they fall into the pit, to their ruin.ESVLet destruction come upon him when he does not know it! And let the net that he hid ensnare him; let him fall into it\u2014to his destruction!NIVMay those who seek my life be disgraced and put to shame; may those who plot my ruin be turned back in dismay.ESVLet them be put to shame and dishonor who seek after my life! Let them be turned back and disappointed who devise evil against me!

rows.label: Contend for Me — 35:1–18
rows.text: "Fight those who fight me." Enemies repay evil for good. Traps without cause.

hebtext.note: "Contend (rîbâ), LORD, with those who contend with me" (v.1). A legal-military psalm. David asks God to take up shield and buckler (v.2), to say to his soul "I am your salvation" (v.3). The enemies set traps without cause (v.7), repaid evil for good (v.12), gloated when David stumbled (v.15). David protests his innocence and asks for vindication.

"Vindicate me in your righteousness (ṣidqâ), LORD my God" (v.24). The psalm moves from plea to confidence. David asks that those who delight in his vindication will praise God continually (v.27). God’s tongue will proclaim his righteousness all day long (v.28).

themes.note: Justice peaks: legal plea for vindication against false accusers. Judgment: God as advocate and warrior.
